concepts.name: Frontstage Disruption
concepts.definition: Disruption to customer-facing building blocks: Value Propositions, Channels, and Customer Relationships. The visible part of the business model that customers experience directly. Changing these shifts how customers perceive and interact with the company.

concepts.name: Backstage Disruption
concepts.definition: Disruption to operational building blocks: Key Resources, Key Activities, Key Partnerships. The invisible infrastructure that enables customer-facing innovation. Often creates competitive advantage that is hard for competitors to see or copy.

concepts.name: Profit Formula Disruption
concepts.definition: Disruption to financial building blocks: Revenue Streams and Cost Structure. Changing how money is made and spent. Often the most powerful form of business model innovation because it directly changes the economics.

concepts.name: Business Model Shift Patterns
concepts.definition: A library of repeatable patterns for improving or transforming business models, organized by which building block changes. Examples: Resource-Driven Shift, Activity-Driven Shift, Partnership-Driven Shift, Channel-Driven Shift, Customer Relationship-Driven Shift, Revenue Model Shift, Cost Structure Shift. Each pattern describes the trigger, mechanism, and examples.

concepts.name: Innovation Culture Design
concepts.definition: Deliberately designing the organizational culture to support exploration. Key elements: psychological safety (safe to fail), tolerance for productive failure, fast iteration cycles, customer obsession, resource autonomy for Explore teams, different recognition/reward systems than Exploit roles. Culture emerges from management systems — change the system to change the culture.

arguments.claim: Most business model shifts follow identifiable patterns — systematic innovation is teachable, not just an art
arguments.evidence: The pattern library catalogs recurring shift types across industries and time. Just as the BMC patterns chapter showed recurring business model archetypes (Long Tail, Multi-Sided Platform, etc.), the shift library shows recurring ways models evolve. This makes business model innovation a discipline that can be learned.

arguments.claim: Culture eats strategy for breakfast on Explore AND Exploit — but they require different cultures
arguments.evidence: Explore requires a culture rewarding experimentation, tolerating productive failure, valuing learning velocity over execution precision. Exploit requires consistency, efficiency, and reliability. One organization cannot optimize for both — it must deliberately build two cultural environments connected by strategic intent.

terminology.definition: A repeatable logic for changing one or more building blocks of a business model, documented with conditions for use and industry examples.
Customer-facing side: Value Propositions + Channels + Customer Relationships + Revenue Streams — what customers see and experience.
Operational side: Key Resources + Key Activities + Key Partnerships + Cost Structure — the invisible engine.
The shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. Critical for Explore because innovation requires surfacing and testing uncertain ideas.

nuance: Shift patterns are inspiration and starting points, not blueprints. Every pattern must be adapted to the specific industry, customer context, and competitive situation.

Culture cannot be 'transformed' by a CEO vision speech — it emerges from the management system (what gets measured, rewarded, and discussed). Change the metrics and resource allocation, and culture follows.
